other_observers: Missy arnold, joyce takamine, another person whose name i'll have to search, Joe Mammoser; YES for 2012 bird(s) Craig Dodson saw it at a later date Dick Filby's birding group on 13 Apr
specific_location: DEVILS KITCHEN TRAIL HEAD, COLORADO NATIONAL MONUMENT, 500 M FROM PARKING LOT
description: BCSP description – Devils Kitchen Trail at Colorado National Monument, Mesa County CO, 09 April 2012  One singing bird was encountered 09 Apr 2012 on the same territory as last year (09 Apr – 18 Jun 2011, m.ob, photos, accepted by CBRC). Bird was out in the open about ten feet off the ground in a tallish shrub (no leaves yet), moving to eye level at times, all gray head and breast, not streaky, a small amount of black around the base of the bill and downward onto the throat area (but the most recent time i saw it, wil Joe Mammoser, there appeared to be a larger area of black on the face), back was brown and streaky. Superficially junco-like in appearance but no obviously white belly, and NO white on the outer rectrices when it moved around (eliminating all junco plumages). Smallish bill was not gray or black but I am color-challenged so can not name the bill color without a close-up view in a spotting scope. The habitat (geography and vegetation) of this area were described last year, and is available on request. NOTE - one bird was seen again near this same area, on GVAS Spring Count by a very exoerienced birder, Brett Walker. One bird was seen on 09 Apr, but two birds were seen on two other occasions since then, 3 observers on one occasion, 2 observers on another occasion. I know of at least 14 observers, including a group of Brits with Dick Filby. First known sighting this year was 09 Apr, and most recent was 12 May. I have two fairly good quality photos to share with CBRC if i can figure out how to inclde them. i might need to simply forward to Faulkner or somebody?Behaviors: fairly shy, except when singing or chasing or being chased by BTSP, JUTI, etcCall: same as all of my recordings, very clear, bouncing ball (accelerating)... Song was conspicuous, as bird was only 15m away and easily viewable with sun almost directly behind me. Song consisted of high sharp, slurred notes accelerating into a rapid trill not unlike a Field Sparrow, but was higher and more mechanical than the latter, a “bouncing Ping-Pong ball” sort of song.Plumage: breeding
